---

# Locality Guide: Pennsylvania Quaker Communities (Late 1700s, c. 1760–1800)

## Jurisdiction Overview

- **Founded:** Pennsylvania granted to William Penn by royal charter in 1681; established 1682 as an intentional Quaker haven. Ratified the U.S. Constitution in 1787.
- **Parent jurisdiction:** British Crown (colonial period), then United States
- **Economy:** Mixed agriculture, milling, trade; Philadelphia was the largest city in colonial America and a major commercial hub
- **Ethnic/religious composition:** Predominantly English Quakers in the core counties, with German Reformed, Lutheran, Anglican, and Presbyterian communities also present
- **Core Quaker counties:** Philadelphia, Chester, Bucks, and Delaware counties had the densest Quaker populations in the colonial and early national periods, per Wikipedia's article on *Quakers in Pennsylvania*
- **Key boundary changes affecting research (see below):** Montgomery County was formed from Philadelphia County in 1784; Delaware County was formed from Chester County in 1789. Records for events before those dates are held by the *parent* counties.
- **No population data** was returned by FamilySearch's place_population tool for this period.

---

## Boundary Changes

| New County | Formed | From | Date |
|---|---|---|---|
| Montgomery County | 1784 | Philadelphia County | Records before 1784 → Philadelphia County |
| Delaware County | 1789 | Chester County | Records before 1789 → Chester County |

If your ancestors lived in what is now Montgomery or Delaware County but you are searching events before 1784/1789, search Philadelphia and Chester County records respectively — not the modern county.

---

## Available Record Types

### ⭐ Quaker Meeting Records — The Primary Source for This Period

This is the crown jewel for late-1700s Pennsylvania Quaker research. Per the FamilySearch Wiki article *Pennsylvania Church Records*, Quaker (Society of Friends) meeting records are among the most detailed religious records in Pennsylvania. Monthly meetings recorded:

- **Births** (registered by the meeting clerk from family reports)
- **Marriages** (Quaker marriage certificates were signed by *all attendees* — a uniquely rich witness list)
- **Deaths and burials**
- **Certificates of Removal** — issued when a member moved from one monthly meeting to another; essential for tracing migration
- **Disownments** — recorded when members violated discipline (marrying outside the meeting, bearing arms, swearing oaths, etc.)
- **Sufferings records** — fines and penalties for refusing militia service, tithes, or oaths

The Quaker meeting structure was hierarchical: **preparative meetings → monthly meetings → quarterly meetings → Philadelphia Yearly Meeting (PYM)**. PYM encompassed most of Pennsylvania, New Jersey, and Delaware. Most genealogically useful records are at the **monthly meeting** level.

**Because Pennsylvania did not mandate statewide civil registration of births and deaths until 1906**, these meeting records are often the *only* documentation of vital events for Quaker families in the late 1700s.

#### Where Quaker Meeting Records Are Held

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore College** (Swarthmore, PA) | Largest collection of American Quaker records; originals and microfilm of most Pennsylvania monthly meeting minutes and registers | In-person; some digitized; microfilm loan possible |
| **Quaker and Special Collections, Haverford College** (Haverford, PA) | Major Quaker manuscript and printed collections | In-person |
| **FamilySearch** | Microfilmed monthly meeting minutes and registers for many PA meetings | See digitization status below |

#### FamilySearch Digitization Status for Quaker Records

**Digitized volume found:**
- **Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes, 1750–1800** — Image Group 001188322, 145 images
  - ⚠️ **Browse-only images.** `recordSearchablePercent: 0`, `fulltextSearchable: false`
  - Must be browsed image by image with no name-search capability
  - Language: English

This is the only digitized Quaker-specific volume returned for Pennsylvania in the 1760–1800 window. FamilySearch has microfilmed additional monthly meeting registers that do not yet appear as digitized/indexed volumes — check the FamilySearch Catalog under the specific county and meeting name.

**Broader church collection on FamilySearch:**
- **Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985** (Collection 1401638) — 1,245,830 records, 982,415 persons, 607,220 images — likely includes selected Quaker meeting records alongside other denominations
  - Access: [https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638)

#### Published Abstract: Hinshaw's Encyclopedia

The FamilySearch Wiki (*Pennsylvania Church Records — Published Quaker Abstracts*) highlights:

> **William Wade Hinshaw, *Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy*, 6 volumes** — abstracts monthly meeting records from Pennsylvania and other states. This is a standard finding aid for Quaker research and partially indexes records that are otherwise only browsable.

Hinshaw's *Encyclopedia* is available in major genealogical libraries, on microfilm at the Family History Library, and partially online through HathiTrust.

---

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)

- **Birth/death registration:** Pennsylvania did **not** mandate statewide registration until **1906**. No civil birth or death records exist for the late 1700s.
- **Marriage registration:** Statewide civil marriage registration did not begin until **1885**.
- **Pre-registration alternatives:** Quaker meeting records (see above) are the primary substitute. See also probate records, church registers of other denominations, and newspaper notices.

**FamilySearch collections overlapping the period:**
| Collection | Date Range | Records | Images | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950 | 1677–1950 | 312,045 | 156,023 | [Collection 1571957](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1571957) |
| Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900 | 1700–1900 | 198,765 | 0 | Index only — no images; [Collection 1502909](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1502909) |

---

### Census Records

- **Federal census:** The first U.S. federal census was **1790** (heads of household only; no ages except free white males). This is the earliest federal enumeration for this period.
- **Pre-1790:** No federal census. Tax lists (see below) are the primary substitute.
- **State census:** Pennsylvania conducted no colonial-era enumeration survives widely. Some county-level militia musters and provincial tax assessments function as substitutes.
- **Access:** 1790 census is fully indexed and online at FamilySearch and Ancestry.

---

### Probate Records

Probate records are particularly valuable for this period because they:
- Name heirs and family relationships not captured elsewhere
- Pre-date civil registration by well over a century
- Often identify religious affiliation

**FamilySearch collection:**
- **Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994** (Collection 1417683) — 893,214 records, 446,607 images
  - Covers the full late-1700s period
  - [https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1417683](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1417683)

Probate records are held at the county level (Orphans' Court / Register of Wills). For Montgomery County events before 1784, search Philadelphia County probate; for Delaware County before 1789, search Chester County.

---

### Land Records

- Pennsylvania is a **state-land state** (metes-and-bounds surveys; no federal land patents apply)
- **Pennsylvania Land Office** (Harrisburg) holds original warrants, surveys, and patents
- County **Recorders of Deeds** hold deed books from county formation
- Pennsylvania State Archives holds colonial-era land records
- Tax lists (see below) serve as a proxy land record for the late 1700s

---

### Tax Records

Pennsylvania county tax lists are a critical substitute for pre-1790 census coverage. They enumerate taxable inhabitants (property owners and renters) and typically record:
- Name, township, and county
- Property type and valuation
- Occupation

**Key years:** Pennsylvania triennial assessments survive from the 1760s onward for many counties. The **1779 and 1780 tax assessments** are particularly complete and well-preserved. These are held at county archives and the Pennsylvania State Archives; some have been microfilmed by FamilySearch.

---

### Military Records

Quaker families present a special challenge here: **Quakers were pacifists** and frequently refused militia service, resulting in **fines and disownments** rather than service records. Check:
- Meeting **sufferings records** (fines for refusing military duty) — held at Friends Historical Library
- **Pennsylvania militia muster rolls** (for non-Quaker neighbors and kin) — Pennsylvania State Archives, Fold3
- **Revolutionary War pension files** — NARA, searchable on Fold3 ([fold3.com](https://www.fold3.com/))
- Members who bore arms were often **disowned** by their meeting — the disownment record names the person and reason

---

### Cemetery Records

Quaker meetinghouses maintained **plain, unmarked or simply marked burial grounds**. Many headstones from the late 1700s carried only initials or no inscription at all. Burial was often recorded in:
- Monthly meeting minutes (death entries)
- Separate burial registers maintained by some meetings

**Online coverage:**
- [FindAGrave.com](https://www.findagrave.com/) — some PA Quaker burial grounds are transcribed, but coverage is uneven for 18th-century sites
- Friends Historical Library holds burial registers for many meetings

---

### Newspaper Records

Philadelphia newspapers (e.g., *Pennsylvania Gazette*, *Pennsylvania Packet*) published marriage notices and death notices in the late 1700s. Some Quaker marriages were announced publicly.

- **Newspapers.com** ([newspapers.com](https://www.newspapers.com/)) — subscription; some 18th-century Philadelphia papers available
- **Accessible Archives** — digitized *Pennsylvania Gazette* and other early Philadelphia papers
- **Chronicling America** (Library of Congress) — free; limited 18th-century PA coverage

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories
| Repository | Relevant Collections | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Pennsylvania Church and Town Records 1708–1985; Probate Records 1683–1994; Civil Marriages 1677–1950; Compiled Marriages 1700–1900; Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes 1750–1800 (browse-only) | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. Federal Census 1790; Pennsylvania records; Quaker collections | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Revolutionary War pension files; PA militia records | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave** | Cemetery transcriptions (uneven 18th-c. coverage) | Free |
| **HathiTrust / Internet Archive** | Hinshaw's *Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy* (some volumes) | Free |

### Physical Repositories
| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore College** (500 College Ave, Swarthmore, PA 19081) | Originals and microfilm of Pennsylvania monthly meeting minutes, registers, certificates of removal, sufferings records — the largest Quaker archive in America | In-person; catalog at swarthmore.edu/friends-historical-library |
| **Quaker and Special Collections, Haverford College** (370 Lancaster Ave, Haverford, PA 19041) | Major Quaker manuscript collection, printed records | In-person |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg) | Colonial and early state land records, county tax assessments, militia records | In-person and online request |
| **Philadelphia City Archives** | Philadelphia County court, probate, and deed records | In-person |
| **Chester County Archives** (West Chester) | Chester and pre-1789 Delaware County records | In-person |
| **Bucks County Historical Society** (Doylestown) | Bucks County records; some Quaker materials | In-person |
| **Family History Library** (Salt Lake City) / FamilySearch Centers | Microfilm of many PA monthly meeting registers; Hinshaw's *Encyclopedia* | In-person; microfilm loan through FamilySearch Centers |

---

## Records NOT Online

The following record types exist but are **not available in any online name-searchable database**:

- Most **monthly meeting minutes and registers** beyond the single Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes volume (1750–1800) on FamilySearch — the bulk of this material is microfilm-only at FHL or physical-only at Swarthmore/Haverford
- **Certificates of Removal** — physically held at Friends Historical Library; some on FamilySearch microfilm but not digitized/indexed
- **Sufferings records** — Friends Historical Library; not online
- **Pennsylvania county tax assessments** (pre-1790) — many are microfilm-only at the Family History Library or physical-only at county archives
- **Pennsylvania Land Office warrants and surveys** — Pennsylvania State Archives; partially indexed via the PA State Archives online catalog but images not universally available

---

## Known Record Losses

- **No significant courthouse fires** destroyed the core Philadelphia, Chester, or Bucks County records in this period (unlike many Southern counties)
- **Quaker meeting records are generally well-preserved** because the Religious Society of Friends maintained careful custody of its records and was an early cooperator with microfilming programs
- Some **smaller preparative meeting records** were never formally deposited and may remain in private family or local meeting custody

---

## Research Tips

1. **Start with Hinshaw's *Encyclopedia*.** Before diving into original meeting records, check all 6 volumes of Hinshaw's *Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy* — it abstracts births, marriages, deaths, and disownments from most Pennsylvania monthly meetings and gives you a searchable entry point.

2. **Identify the correct monthly meeting first.** Quaker records are organized by *meeting*, not by county or township. Use a map of Philadelphia Yearly Meeting's constituent monthly meetings to find which meeting covered your ancestor's township. The FHL catalog is organized by meeting name.

3. **Trace certificates of removal aggressively.** When a Quaker family moved, the origin meeting issued a Certificate of Removal to the destination meeting. These certificates name family members and travel between meetings — they are migration records, not just membership transfers. A chain of removals can follow a family across multiple states.

4. **Check disownment records even for "good" ancestors.** Disownments appear in meeting minutes and name the person and the offense. Common late-1700s offenses include marrying outside the meeting (often to a non-Quaker or a cousin), bearing arms during the Revolution, and swearing oaths. These records survive even when the person subsequently reconciled.

5. **Boundary awareness is critical.** Montgomery County (from Philadelphia Co., 1784) and Delaware County (from Chester Co., 1789) mean that events before those dates must be searched in the parent county for civil/probate records — but the *Quaker meeting* boundary is separate from the civil county boundary. A meeting may predate county formation.

6. **The 1790 federal census is your first census anchor** — use it to locate the household, then work backward with tax lists (1779–1789), meeting records, and probate.

7. **Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes (FamilySearch, browse-only).** The single digitized Quaker volume on FamilySearch (Image Group 001188322) covers PYM minutes 1750–1800 in 145 images. It must be browsed page by page — there is no name index. It records decisions at the yearly meeting level, not individual vital events.

8. **For marriage evidence:** Quaker marriage certificates list every witness who signed — often 30–80 community members. The original certificate was kept by the couple; a copy was recorded in the monthly meeting minutes. These witnesses lists can identify extended kin and community networks even without a direct vital record.

Now I'll run the full parallel survey.Good results — now let me read the Allegheny County wiki page directly and run a targeted wiki search on Pennsylvania vital records and occupational records.I now have enough to compile a comprehensive guide. Here it is:

---

# Pittsburgh, Allegheny County — Locality Research Guide
**Target Period: Late 1800s (ca. 1870–1900) · Focus: Steel Industry & Occupational Sources**

---

## Jurisdictional Context

Pittsburgh is a city in **Allegheny County, Pennsylvania** (county formed 1788), seated at the confluence of the Allegheny, Monongahela, and Ohio rivers. During the target period it was the undisputed center of American steel manufacturing — Carnegie's mills and dozens of allied firms drew enormous waves of immigrant labor: Irish, German, Polish, Slovak, Croatian, and Italian workers settled the city and the surrounding Mon Valley mill towns. That industrial character shapes the record landscape in two important ways:

1. **Occupational records are unusually rich** — company records, union rolls, and factory inspector reports exist here that you won't find for most U.S. counties.
2. **The immigrant profile** means ethnic parish registers are often the *only* source for vital events before 1906, because Pennsylvania did not require statewide civil registration of births and deaths until that year. Allegheny County maintained its own local registration beginning c. 1870, but coverage is incomplete.

---

## Record Classes, Availability & Access

### 🏭 Occupational & Industrial Records *(the unique resource layer)*

| Source | What It Documents | Repository / Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Company employment records** | Hire dates, job titles, wages, nationality | Fragmentary; surviving records at the **Senator John Heinz History Center** (Pittsburgh) and the **Rivers of Steel National Heritage Area archives** (Homestead) |
| **Union membership lists** | Names, lodge numbers, sometimes addresses | Rivers of Steel archives; United Steelworkers archives (Pittsburgh) |
| **City directories** (annual) | Occupation, employer, street address | **Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh** holds Pittsburgh city directories from the 1810s onward — coverage is annual through this period. Also on Ancestry.com |
| **Pennsylvania State Factory Inspector Reports** | Mill-by-mill worker counts, accident records, nationalities | Published annually from 1889; held at **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg); some volumes digitized via HathiTrust |
| **Employer accident records / coroner inquests** | Deaths and injuries at mills | Allegheny County Coroner records; Heinz History Center |

> **Access note:** The Heinz History Center and Rivers of Steel archives require in-person or written requests; neither collection is fully digitized online.

---

### 📜 Vital Records

Pennsylvania had **no statewide birth or death registration** in the late 1800s. Allegheny County began its own local registration around 1870, but coverage is spotty. Your primary substitutes are:

- **Church registers** (see below) — the most reliable vital source for this period
- **Death certificates begin statewide 1906** — FamilySearch has *Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968* (4.2 million records, fully indexed with images) but this is *outside* your target window; use it to trace descendants or confirm earlier data
- **Marriage records** have been kept at the county courthouse since the colonial era

**FamilySearch indexed collections covering this period:**

| Collection | Date Range | Records | Images | Access Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985 | 1708–1985 | 1,245,830 | 607,220 | Indexed + images |
| Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885–1950 | 1885–1950 | 524,189 | 312,045 | Indexed + images |
| Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950 | 1677–1950 | 312,045 | 156,023 | Indexed + images |
| Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900 | 1700–1900 | 198,765 | *none* | Index only — no images |
| Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994 | 1683–1994 | 893,214 | 446,607 | Indexed + images |

*(Source: FamilySearch collections_search for Pennsylvania, filtered 1860–1910)*

---

### 🏛️ Church Records *(critical for immigrant steel workers)*

Because civil registration was absent or incomplete, **ethnic parish registers are the highest-priority vital source** for this population. The FamilySearch wiki confirms that Catholic parishes served specific nationalities:

- **Polish** — St. Stanislaus Kostka, others in the South Side and Strip District
- **Slovak** — St. John Nepomucene, St. Elizabeth
- **Croatian/Slovenian** — St. Nicholas Croatian, St. Joseph Slovenian
- **Italian** — St. Peter's and others
- **Irish & German** — well-established Catholic and Protestant congregations predating the steel boom

**Where to find them:**
- FamilySearch's *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985* (1.2 million records) is the starting point
- Diocese of Pittsburgh Archives holds sacramental registers not yet microfilmed
- Individual parish archives — contact the parish directly
- Many Eastern Catholic (Byzantine rite) Slovak/Ruthenian registers survive at their own diocesan archives

---

### 👥 Federal Census Records

The 1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses all record **occupation** for each person — a direct link to industrial employment. The 1890 census was destroyed; the 1900 census is the closest substitute.

- **Mortality schedules** (1870 and 1880) record deaths in the 12 months before enumeration, including cause of death — valuable for tracing industrial accident fatalities
- All available on FamilySearch and Ancestry; fully indexed

---

### 🌐 Immigration & Naturalization Records

The Allegheny County steel workforce was predominantly immigrant. Key records:

| Record Type | What It Contains | Where Held |
|---|---|---|
| **Naturalization petitions & declarations** (pre-1906) | Name, country of birth, arrival date, court | Allegheny County courthouse; some at **NARA Philadelphia** (Mid-Atlantic Region) |
| **Passenger arrival lists** | Name, age, origin, sometimes destination contact | NARA; indexed on Ancestry and FamilySearch |
| **Passports / border crossing cards** | Later in period (1890s onward) | NARA |

> Pre-1906 naturalizations could be filed in *any* court of record — check not just Common Pleas but also Quarter Sessions, Orphans' Court, and federal district court for the Western District of Pennsylvania.

*(Source: FamilySearch wiki, "Allegheny County, Pennsylvania Genealogy — Immigration Records")*

---

### 📰 Newspapers

Pittsburgh had robust newspaper coverage of the steel industry — labor strikes (especially the 1892 Homestead Strike), mill accidents, and immigration are extensively reported. Occupational obituaries frequently name employer, department, and years of service.

- **Pittsburgh Post-Gazette** and **Pittsburgh Dispatch** — available on Newspapers.com (curated external link returned by FS)
- **Ethnic-language press** — Slovak, Polish, and Croatian newspapers published in Pittsburgh; some on Chronicling America (Library of Congress, free)

---

### 🏦 Probate & Property Records

- **Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994** on FamilySearch: 893,000+ records with images — covers Allegheny County wills, estate inventories, and administrations through this period
- Estate inventories can document tools, wages owed, and personal property consistent with industrial occupations
- **Property tax records** (assessment lists) survive at Allegheny County and the Pennsylvania State Archives — useful when other records are sparse

---

### ⚔️ Military Records

For the late 1800s this covers primarily **Civil War veterans** (1861–65) still living in Pittsburgh and the **Spanish-American War** (1898). Veterans' pension files (NARA) often contain family data, dates, and place of birth. Fold3.com (curated external link) is the main access point for service and pension records.

---

## Key Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | Strengths for This Research | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Senator John Heinz History Center** (Pittsburgh) | Industrial records, company archives, immigration, Pittsburgh photographs | In-person; some finding aids online at heinzhistorycenter.org |
| **Rivers of Steel National Heritage Area** (Homestead) | Carnegie Steel / US Steel company records, union archives | In-person / written requests |
| **Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh** | City directories (annual, 1810s–), local newspapers, local history collection | In-person; some digitized |
| **Allegheny County Courthouse** | Naturalization records, marriage licenses, deeds, court files | In-person; Register of Wills online at county website |
| **NARA Philadelphia** (Mid-Atlantic Region) | Federal naturalization records, passenger lists, federal court records | In-person / online NARA catalog |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg) | Factory inspector reports, state agency records, tax lists | In-person; search catalog at digitalarchives.state.pa.us |
| **Diocese of Pittsburgh Archives** | Catholic parish registers not yet microfilmed | Written request |
| **FamilySearch.org** | Indexed marriages, probate, church records, census | Free online |
| **Ancestry.com** | Census, city directories, passenger lists (curated FS external link) | Subscription |
| **Find A Grave / BillionGraves** | Cemetery inscriptions — mill towns have large ethnic cemeteries | Free online |

---

## Research Strategy Notes

1. **Start with the 1880 census** — it's fully indexed, records occupations, and falls squarely in the steel boom. Note the ward/district so you can cross-reference city directories for the same address.
2. **City directories are your occupational backbone.** Annual Pittsburgh directories list employer and occupation; use them to track career moves between census years.
3. **Follow the ethnic parish.** Once you know a worker's nationality, identify the corresponding Catholic or Lutheran parish for that neighborhood and time — those registers will carry births, marriages, and deaths the civil records missed.
4. **Don't neglect the Homestead Strike records (1892).** Surviving Carnegie Steel employment lists, court records, and newspaper coverage from this event are unusually detailed and can place specific workers at specific mills.
5. **The Heinz History Center and Rivers of Steel should be contacted directly** — their finding aids are not fully online, and a reference archivist can tell you whether records for a specific mill or employer survive.

---

**Coverage gaps to note:** No statewide death records before 1906; company employment records are fragmentary and survival is uneven by firm; the 1890 federal census is destroyed. Church registers are the essential bridge across these gaps.

# Philadelphia, Pennsylvania — 1793 Yellow Fever Epidemic
## Locality Research Guide

**Target period:** 1793 (epidemic August–November), with flanking years 1788–1800
**Jurisdictional note:** Philadelphia existed as both a **city** (incorporated 1682) and a co-terminus **county** (Philadelphia County, 1682–). In 1793, it was the national capital with a population of roughly 50,000. The yellow fever epidemic killed approximately 5,000 residents — about one in ten — between August and November. Pennsylvania did **not** have statewide civil registration of births or deaths in 1793; that began only in 1906. Church records are therefore the cornerstone of vital-event documentation for this period.

---

## ⭐ Epidemic-Specific Sources (Start Here)

These record classes are unique to the 1793 outbreak and do not exist for ordinary years:

| Source | What It Contains | Where Held | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mathew Carey,** *A Short Account of the Malignant Fever…* (1793, 4th ed.) | Appendix lists names of the dead by neighborhood | Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP); digitized via HathiTrust / Internet Archive | Free online |
| **Minutes of the Committee of Philadelphia** (the citizen relief committee chaired by Mayor Clarkson) | Daily minutes naming the sick, dead, and recipients of relief; lists of orphaned children | HSP manuscript collection | Physical / microfilm; inquire with HSP |
| **Bush Hill Hospital records** | Admissions and deaths at the epidemic's improvised hospital | HSP | Physical; finding aid available |
| **Benjamin Rush's casebooks and correspondence** | Patient names, neighborhood observations | Library Company of Philadelphia; HSP | Physical; partial transcriptions published |
| **Philadelphia Orphan Society records** | Children orphaned by the epidemic; guardian and family names | Philadelphia City Archives | Physical |

> **Research tip:** The FamilySearch Wiki page *Philadelphia Genealogy* (https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Philadelphia_Genealogy#Yellow_Fever_1793) confirms all five of these source types and names HSP and Philadelphia City Archives as the primary custodians.

---

## 1. Church Burial Records
**Importance: Critical** — the only contemporaneous death registration in 1793.

Pennsylvania had no civil death registration until 1906. Church burial registers are the sole systematic record of when and where individuals died. The epidemic's speed caused some registers to spike dramatically in August–November 1793 entries, making the outbreak visible register-by-register.

**Key Philadelphia congregations with 18th-century registers:**

| Congregation | Denomination | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Christ Church | Anglican/Episcopal | One of the city's oldest; extensive burial register; many civic leaders |
| Gloria Dei (Old Swedes' Church) | Lutheran/Episcopal | Founded 1700; South Philadelphia neighborhood |
| St. Peter's Church | Episcopal | Founded 1758; separate register from Christ Church |
| St. Mary's Roman Catholic | Catholic | Arch Street; large Irish and German congregation |
| Mikveh Israel | Jewish | Oldest Jewish congregation in Philadelphia |
| First Presbyterian | Presbyterian | Many Scots-Irish families |
| Arch Street Friends Meeting | Quaker | Quakers kept meticulous monthly meeting records |
| Zion Lutheran / St. Michael's | German Lutheran | Large German-speaking community |
| St. Joseph's | Roman Catholic | Oldest Catholic parish; Willing's Alley |

**FamilySearch access:**
- **Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985** (Collection 1401638) — 1,245,830 indexed records with 607,220 images. Many Philadelphia church registers are included. Search at: https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638
- The Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania (GSP) has compiled indexes to many Philadelphia church records; check their *Pennsylvania Genealogical Magazine* volumes.

**Physical repositories:** Original registers are split among individual congregations, HSP, Presbyterian Historical Society, Friends Historical Library (Swarthmore), and the Quaker collections at Haverford College.

---

## 2. FamilySearch Digitized Collections (1750–1820 overlap)

| Collection | Date Range | Records | Images | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985 | 1708–1985 | 1,245,830 | 607,220 | **Indexed + images** — primary source for pre-1906 vital events |
| Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994 | 1683–1994 | 893,214 | 446,607 | **Indexed + images** — estates of epidemic victims; see §4 |
| Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950 | 1677–1950 | 312,045 | 156,023 | **Indexed + images** — covers 1793 period |
| Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900 | 1700–1900 | 198,765 | 0 | **Index only, no images** — use to find names, then seek original |

> **Note:** Pennsylvania Death Certificates (Collection 2255317) runs 1906–1968 and does **not** cover 1793. Do not expect a death certificate for epidemic victims.

---

## 3. Census Records

| Census | Coverage | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **1790 Federal Census** | Heads of household only | Taken just before the epidemic; establishes household composition and neighborhood. The original Philadelphia schedules survive. |
| **1800 Federal Census** | Heads of household | Post-epidemic snapshot; missing names may indicate epidemic mortality or flight. Compare to 1790 to identify households dissolved or relocated. |
| Pennsylvania State Tax Lists, 1780s–1790s | Property owners | Not a census, but can corroborate residence by ward/district |

No federal mortality schedules exist before 1850. The 1793 epidemic predates that series entirely.

---

## 4. Probate Records
**Importance: High** — epidemic deaths generated a surge of estate filings in late 1793–1794.

Philadelphia County probate records (wills, letters of administration, inventories) are held by the **Register of Wills, Philadelphia City Hall** and have been partially microfilmed by FamilySearch.

- **Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994** (Collection 1417683) — 893,214 indexed records with images: https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1417683
- Wills proved in fall 1793 and early 1794 will name surviving family members (spouses, children, executors), directly documenting family structure at the moment of death.
- Estate inventories list household goods, debts, and neighbors who witnessed — useful for reconstructing community networks in an epidemic-affected neighborhood.

---

## 5. Land and Tax Records
**Importance: Moderate** — locates families geographically before and after the epidemic.

- **Philadelphia City/County Deed Books** — held at the **Philadelphia City Archives**; partially indexed. Track property transfers in the epidemic's aftermath (sales by estates, widows, orphan guardians).
- **Pennsylvania Tax Records, 1762–1794** — many volumes available on FamilySearch and Ancestry; document residents by ward in the years surrounding 1793.
- **Ward and District Maps** — the epidemic struck different wards differently. Identifying which ward a family lived in lets you cross-reference mortality cluster data from the Carey and Committee records.

---

## 6. Newspapers
**Importance: High for this specific epidemic** — Philadelphia papers published weekly and daily death lists during the outbreak.

| Newspaper | Notes |
|---|---|
| *Dunlap's American Daily Advertiser* | Published death notices daily during the epidemic |
| *Federal Gazette* | Active during 1793; covered epidemic |
| *General Advertiser* (later *Aurora*) | Benjamin Franklin Bache's paper; covered epidemic politics |

- **Newspapers.com** and **Readex America's Historical Newspapers** hold digitized Philadelphia papers from this period.
- Death notices often name survivors, relationship, and cause.
- Many papers suspended publication at the epidemic's height (September–October 1793) — gaps in a run are themselves evidence of crisis.

---

## 7. Military and Revolutionary War Records

Many Philadelphia families in 1793 had heads of household who were Revolutionary War veterans. Their pension files (NARA) include family depositions filed in the 1810s–1830s that name wives, children, and marriage dates — useful for reconstructing families whose 1793 records are sparse.

- **Fold3 / NARA RG 15** — pension files; search by name
- **Pennsylvania Archives, Series 2, 5, 6** — published muster rolls; many Philadelphia units

---

## 8. Immigration and Naturalization

Philadelphia was the primary U.S. port of entry in 1793. Some epidemic victims had arrived only months earlier.

- **NARA Philadelphia District passenger lists** — pre-1820 lists are sparse and incomplete for this port; gaps are expected.
- **Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950** (Collection 1571957) — 312,045 indexed records: https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1571957
- Naturalization records pre-1906 can appear in any Pennsylvania court; Philadelphia Common Pleas and District Court records are at the **Philadelphia City Archives** and **NARA Mid-Atlantic Region** (Philadelphia).

---

## 9. Key Repositories and Access

| Repository | Holdings Relevant to 1793 | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP)**, Philadelphia | Committee minutes, Rush papers, Bush Hill records, church register transcriptions, Carey's *Short Account* | Reading room by appointment; findingaids.hsp.org |
| **Philadelphia City Archives** | Orphan Society, deed books, court records | Appointment required; phila.gov/city-archives |
| **FamilySearch.org** | Church & town records, probate, marriages (see §2) | Free; online |
| **Ancestry.com** | Federal census (1790, 1800), tax lists, newspapers | Subscription; also free at many libraries |
| **Presbyterian Historical Society**, Philadelphia | Presbyterian church registers | By appointment |
| **Friends Historical Library**, Swarthmore College | Quaker monthly meeting records | Reading room |
| **Haverford College Quaker Collection** | Quaker records | Reading room |
| **NARA Mid-Atlantic Region**, Philadelphia | Federal court records, naturalizations, passenger lists | Appointment; also online via Ancestry/Fold3 |
| **Library Company of Philadelphia** | 18th-century imprints, Rush manuscript | Reading room |

---

## 10. Research Strategy for Epidemic-Affected Families

1. **Start with Mathew Carey's list** (free on HathiTrust) to confirm whether your ancestor's name appears among the documented dead.
2. **Check the church burial register** for the denomination most likely associated with your family — the spike in September–October 1793 entries is unmistakable.
3. **Pull the probate file** if the person died — the will or administration file names survivors and executors.
4. **Compare 1790 and 1800 census entries** — household members present in 1790 but absent in 1800, combined with a matching burial register entry, builds a strong inference of epidemic mortality.
5. **Search Philadelphia newspapers** for death notices in August–November 1793.
6. **Check the Orphan Society records** if children survived an epidemic death — guardianship filings name both deceased parents.
7. **Note absences as evidence**: if a family fled (thousands did), they may appear in records of surrounding counties (Montgomery, Chester, Bucks) during fall 1793 before returning.

---

## Coverage Gaps to Note

- **No civil death registration** in Pennsylvania until 1906. There is no government death record for any 1793 victim.
- **Bush Hill hospital records** are fragmentary — not all admissions were documented.
- **African American families**: The Free African Society under Absalom Jones and Richard Allen organized much of the nursing and burial during the epidemic. Their records (held at the African Episcopal Church of St. Thomas and Mother Bethel AME) document some Black Philadelphians not captured in white church registers. Mathew Carey's account initially blamed Black Philadelphians unfairly; Jones and Allen published a rebuttal (*A Narrative of the Proceedings of the Black People…*, 1794) that contains additional names.
- **The 1793 Philadelphia city directory** (published by James Hardie) lists householders by occupation and address — useful for locating families in the pre-epidemic city. Not a genealogical record per se, but a strong locator tool.

# Chicago / Cook County, Illinois Locality Guide
## Families Affected by the Great Chicago Fire, 1871
*Period of focus: c. 1840–1900*

---

## ⚠️ The Core Research Problem: Mass Record Destruction

The **Great Chicago Fire (October 8–10, 1871)** burned 3.3 square miles of the city, killed ~300 people, and left more than 100,000 residents homeless. Critically for genealogists, it **destroyed the Cook County Courthouse**, which housed:

- Deeds and property records
- Birth and death registers
- Probate files
- Court records (civil and criminal)
- Tax rolls

This means **virtually no Cook County civil vital records exist before October 1871**. Illinois statewide vital registration didn't even begin until 1916, so researchers are doubly disadvantaged for pre-fire births and deaths. Every search strategy below must account for this gap.

---

## Jurisdictional Context

| Detail | Value |
|---|---|
| County formed | 1831, from unorganized territory |
| Standard place | Cook, Illinois, United States |
| FamilySearch scope | Illinois state level for collections |

Cook County records before 1871 exist only as **survivors, copies, or substitutes** — not as intact original series. Post-1871 records largely restart from scratch.

---

## Record Classes — Availability & Access

### 1. 🔥 SUBSTITUTE SOURCES FOR PRE-1871 (Priority Research)

These survived the fire because originals were **not held locally**:

#### Federal Census Schedules
- **1840, 1850, 1860, 1870** — all survived; originals were held in Washington, D.C.
- The **1870 census** is particularly critical: it is the last pre-fire snapshot of who lived in Chicago and where
- The **1890 census** was largely destroyed in a separate 1921 fire — a second gap
- **Access:** Indexed and searchable on FamilySearch and Ancestry.com

#### City Directories (1839 onward)
- Published annually; list heads of household, occupation, and street address
- **Survived** because printed copies were held in multiple locations
- Particularly useful for locating families between census years and pinpointing neighborhoods before and after the fire
- **Access:** The **Newberry Library** (Chicago) holds the most complete run; many years are digitized via Ancestry.com and Archive.org

#### Naturalization Records
- Circuit Court and Superior Court naturalizations **survived from the 1840s onward**
- These predate the fire and are a key source for immigrant families
- **Access:** FamilySearch microfilm holdings; Illinois Regional Archives Depository (IRAD) at Northeastern Illinois University

#### Reconstructed Deeds — Burnt Record Act (1872)
- Illinois passed the **Burnt Record Act of 1872** requiring property owners to re-register their deeds
- These re-recordings document pre-fire property ownership and are a partial reconstruction of the lost deed books
- FamilySearch holds **2 digitized volumes** covering this material:
  - **Pre-fire County Court Records (reconstructed copies), 1865–1871** — Image Group 009982110 — 38 images, **0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable** → must be browsed image by image
  - **Post-fire Property Re-recordings, 1871–1880** — Image Group 009982111 — 112 images, **41% name-indexed** → partially searchable by record_search, remainder browse-only
- **Physical originals:** Cook County Recorder of Deeds; **Chicago Title and Trust Company** collection at the **Chicago History Museum**

#### Insurance Records
- The **Chicago Title and Trust Company collection** at the **Chicago History Museum** is one of the most important substitute sources for pre-fire property ownership
- Insurance surveys documented buildings and owners before the fire
- **Access:** Chicago History Museum (in-person or by correspondence); not digitized online

#### Church Registers
- Parishes that survived or were re-established often reconstructed their pre-fire registers from memory, correspondence, and duplicate books
- Catholic, Lutheran, Episcopal, and other denominations all have holdings
- **Access:** **Archdiocese of Chicago's Joseph Cardinal Bernardin Archives** (Catholic); individual denominational archives; FamilySearch microfilm

---

### 2. POST-1871 VITAL RECORDS (Civil Registration Restarts)

Cook County began local civil registration immediately after the fire. FamilySearch holds three major indexed collections:

| Collection | FamilySearch ID | Records | Date Range | Access |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cook County Birth Certificates | 2024189 | 1,450,000 | 1871–1940 | Indexed + images |
| Cook County Death Records | 1913456 | 980,000 | 1871–1947 | Indexed + images |
| Cook County Marriage Records | 1756321 | 620,000 | 1871–1920 | Indexed + images |

All three are **name-indexed and searchable** on FamilySearch. Physical originals are at the **Cook County Clerk's office**.

> **Research note:** Births and deaths from the first months after the fire (late 1871) may be incomplete as the registration system was being rebuilt. If a record is missing, try church registers for the same period.

---

### 3. CHURCH RECORDS

- **FamilySearch Collection:** *Illinois, Cook County, Chicago, Church Records, 1833–1925* (ID 1803978) — **285,000 records**, indexed + images
- Covers multiple denominations; starts before the fire, making this the primary source for pre-1871 vital events
- **Archdiocese of Chicago's Joseph Cardinal Bernardin Archives** holds Catholic parish registers, including reconstructed pre-fire records
- Church registers often pre-date civil registration by decades and are the **only vital record source** for many families before 1871

---

### 4. CENSUS RECORDS (Summary)

| Year | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1840 | ✅ Survived | Chicago's earliest federal census |
| 1850 | ✅ Survived | First census with all household members named |
| 1860 | ✅ Survived | Pre-fire Chicago population boom |
| 1870 | ✅ Survived | **Critical** — last pre-fire snapshot |
| 1880 | ✅ Survived | First post-fire census; shows rebuilt households |
| 1890 | ❌ Largely destroyed | Separate 1921 fire at NARA |
| 1900 | ✅ Survived | Full reconstruction era view |

Illinois also conducted **state censuses**, though coverage is fragmentary. The Illinois State Archives in Springfield holds what survives.

---

### 5. PROBATE RECORDS

- Pre-1871 probate files were destroyed with the courthouse
- Post-1871 probate is held at the **Cook County Circuit Court Archives**
- For pre-fire estates, check federal census (to document assets/household) and Burnt Record Act re-recordings (property)
- **IRAD at Northeastern Illinois University** holds many Cook County court and probate records post-1871

---

### 6. MILITARY RECORDS

- Federal service records, pension files, and draft registrations are **not held locally** and survived the fire entirely
- **Civil War records** (1861–1865) are at NARA and on Fold3
- Illinois adjutant general records are at the **Illinois State Archives** (Springfield)
- Many Chicago-area men served in Illinois regiments — unit rosters and muster rolls can place a man in Chicago before the fire

---

### 7. IMMIGRATION & NATURALIZATION

- Chicago was a major immigrant gateway (German, Irish, Scandinavian, Polish, Italian, Eastern European communities)
- Pre-fire naturalizations from Circuit and Superior Courts **survived** from the 1840s
- Passenger lists (NARA) were never held locally and survived fully — searchable on Ancestry.com and FamilySearch
- Post-1906 naturalization records are at NARA's Chicago Field Office

---

### 8. NEWSPAPERS

- The **Chicago Tribune** (founded 1847) published fire victim lists, displaced persons notices, relief rolls, and recovery accounts in October–November 1871 — one of the most direct sources for fire survivors
- **Newspapers.com** and the **Newberry Library** hold historical Chicago newspaper runs
- Obituaries and death notices from 1870s papers can substitute for missing vital records

---

### 9. LAND & PROPERTY RECORDS

- Pre-fire: Burnt Record Act re-recordings (FamilySearch Image Groups 009982110 and 009982111, above) and Chicago Title and Trust collection
- Post-fire: Cook County Recorder of Deeds (deeds restart 1871+)
- Federal land patents (Illinois was a federal land state) are at the Bureau of Land Management's General Land Office website — never held locally and fully intact

---

## Key Repositories

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Newberry Library** (Chicago) | City directories (1839+), maps, manuscripts, newspapers | In-person; some digitized |
| **Chicago History Museum** | Insurance records, Chicago Title and Trust collection, photographs, personal papers, fire-related ephemera | In-person; some finding aids online |
| **IRAD — Northeastern Illinois University** | Cook County court, probate, and government records post-1871 | In-person |
| **Archdiocese of Chicago, Bernardin Archives** | Catholic parish registers, including reconstructed pre-fire records | By appointment |
| **Cook County Clerk's Office** | Vital records (births, deaths, marriages) from 1871 | In-person or request by mail |
| **Illinois State Archives (Springfield)** | Military records, state census fragments, land patents, naturalization index | In-person; partial online |
| **NARA — Chicago Field Office** | Federal court records, post-1906 naturalization, some immigration | In-person |
| **FamilySearch.org** | Church records, birth/death/marriage certificates (1871+), microfilmed volumes | Free online |

---

## External Resources (FamilySearch-Curated)

The following sites returned for Cook County in this period (6 resources total):
- **Ancestry.com** — U.S. Federal Census Collection; city directories; Burnt Record Act indexes
- **FindAGrave.com** — Cemetery records (Chicago cemeteries escaped the fire and are intact)
- **Newspapers.com** — Historical Chicago newspapers including fire-era issues
- **Fold3.com** — Military service records for Civil War–era residents
- **NARA (archives.gov)** — Federal court, naturalization, passenger list originals

---

## Research Strategy: Fire-Affected Families

Follow this sequence when the pre-fire paper trail goes cold:

1. **Start with 1870 federal census** — last pre-fire snapshot; establishes names, ages, birthplaces, neighbors
2. **Check 1880 census** — first post-fire; shows who survived, who moved, family reconstitution
3. **Search city directories 1865–1875** — annual volumes bracket the fire and show address changes
4. **Search Chicago Tribune, Oct–Nov 1871** — fire victim lists, relief rolls, missing persons notices
5. **Search church registers** — FamilySearch Collection 1803978 (285,000 records, 1833–1925); Bernardin Archives for Catholic families
6. **Search Burnt Record Act re-recordings** — FamilySearch Image Group 009982111 (41% indexed) for property owners
7. **Check naturalization records** — survived from 1840s; key for immigrant families
8. **Search cemetery records on FindAGrave** — Chicago's major cemeteries (Rosehill, Graceland, Calvary) are intact and predate the fire

> *Sources: FamilySearch Wiki, "Cook County, Illinois Genealogy — Record Loss and the Great Fire" and "Repositories"; FamilySearch Wiki, "Illinois Vital Records — Statewide Registration and Substitute Records"; Wikipedia, "Great Chicago Fire."*

---

# Louisiana Locality Guide — French Colonial Period (c. 1699–1769)

## Jurisdictional Context

Louisiana was founded as a French colony in 1699 and formally organized under **New France**. New Orleans was established in 1718. France governed the colony through the **Superior Council (Conseil Supérieur)**, with the Catholic Church as the only legally recognized religion. In 1762 the **Treaty of Fontainebleau** secretly ceded the territory west of the Mississippi to Spain; Spanish administration became effective around 1769.

**What this means for your research:** The "1700s" spans *two* colonial regimes — French (c. 1699–1769) and early Spanish (1769 onward). If your research extends into the 1770s–1790s, you will cross into Spanish colonial records, which are a continuous research trail from the same repositories but written in Spanish rather than French. This guide focuses on the French period (to ~1769), with notes where the transition is relevant.

**Geographic structure:** Colonial Louisiana used *districts and posts*, not counties. Key jurisdictions include the New Orleans area, Natchitoches Post, Pointe Coupée, Mobile District, and Illinois Country (upper Louisiana). Records are organized by these posts, not by the modern parish system.

**Language warning:** The overwhelming majority of French colonial records are in **18th-century French** (administrative, notarial) and **Latin** (church sacramental acts). Researchers must be able to read these languages and the period handwriting (French secretary hand). Consider engaging a specialist paleographer for difficult documents.

---

## Record Survey by Type

### 1. 🏆 Catholic Parish Registers — PRIMARY SOURCE
*Baptisms, Marriages, Burials (BMDs)*

The Catholic Church was the **only** source of vital registration during the French colonial period. The *Code Noir* (Black Code) mandated Catholic baptism of all enslaved persons, making parish registers the primary source for enslaved ancestors as well.

- **St. Louis Cathedral, New Orleans** — sacramental registers survive from **1720**, making these the oldest continuous parish records in what is now the United States.
- Other posts (Natchitoches, Pointe Coupée, etc.) maintained their own registers; survival varies by location.
- **FamilySearch collection:** [Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1612905) (Collection 1612905) — ~987,000 records with ~493,000 images. This collection starts in **1718** and directly covers the French colonial period. *(Access level: indexed records + images available online at FamilySearch.)*
- **Original registers** are also held at the **Louisiana Historical Center** (Old U.S. Mint, New Orleans) and the **Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives**.

---

### 2. Superior Council Records (Conseil Supérieur)
*Court proceedings, land grants, contracts, estate inventories*

The Superior Council was the colony's governing judicial and administrative body. Its records are among the most genealogically rich sources for the French period, documenting:
- **Land concessions** (grants of land to settlers)
- **Estate inventories and successions** (probate-equivalent)
- **Civil and criminal court proceedings**
- **Contracts and commercial transactions**

These records are held at the **Louisiana Historical Center** (Old U.S. Mint, New Orleans) and have been **partially digitized**. This is a key in-person repository for serious colonial research. *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Louisiana Genealogy — French Colonial Records.")*

---

### 3. Notarial Archives
*Wills, marriage contracts, property sales, manumissions*

New Orleans hosts **the oldest continuous notarial office in North America**. Colonial notarial acts from the **1730s onward** document:
- Marriage settlements (*contrats de mariage*)
- Wills and codicils
- Property sales and donations
- Manumission documents for enslaved people

**Repository:** [New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center](https://www.notarialarchives.org/) — open to public researchers. *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Louisiana Land and Property — Colonial Land Grants.")*

---

### 4. Colonial Census Rolls (Dénombrements)
*Household enumerations*

France conducted periodic census enumerations of the Louisiana colony. These list heads of household, family members, servants, and enslaved people — invaluable for placing families in time and place before other records solidify.

- **Repository:** **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM)**, Aix-en-Provence, France — holds administrative correspondence and census rolls for the colony. ANOM has digitized significant portions of its colonial holdings; check their online portal ([anom.archivesnationales.culture.gouv.fr](https://anom.archivesnationales.culture.gouv.fr)). *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Louisiana Genealogy — French Colonial Records.")*
- No FamilySearch-indexed collection specifically for colonial Louisiana census rolls was returned in this survey — treat this as a gap requiring direct contact with ANOM or the Louisiana State Archives.

---

### 5. Probate Records
*Estate inventories, successions, guardianships*

- **FamilySearch collection:** [Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2078341) (Collection 2078341) — ~456,000 records with ~228,000 images. Coverage begins **1756**, overlapping the final decade of the French period. *(Access level: indexed records + images available online.)*
- Earlier colonial probate-equivalent records (pre-1756) are found within the **Superior Council records** at the Louisiana Historical Center (see above) — treat these as one continuous record trail across the two repositories.

---

### 6. Land Records
*Concessions, grants, surveys*

French land grants (*concessions*) were made under French legal tradition (not English common law). After the Louisiana Purchase, many colonial-era grants were adjudicated by U.S. authorities.

- **American State Papers: Public Lands** series documents French and Spanish colonial land claims — available through the Library of Congress and Ancestry.com.
- **Louisiana State Land Office** holds land grant records.
- Notarial archives (above) also document land transactions from the 1730s onward.
- *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Louisiana Land and Property — Colonial Land Grants.")*

---

### 7. French Colonial Administrative Records
*Correspondence, military rosters, passenger lists, immigration records*

- **ANOM (Aix-en-Provence, France)** is the central repository for France's colonial administrative records. Holds:
  - Governor and intendant correspondence
  - Military musters and officer lists
  - Passenger lists of colonists sent to Louisiana (including the *cassette girls*/filles à la cassette and Company of the Indies settlers, 1718–1731)
- Many of these have been transcribed and published by Louisiana genealogical societies (see Societies, below).

---

### 8. Records That Do NOT Apply to the French Period

| Record type | Why not applicable |
|---|---|
| Federal census (1790+) | Louisiana not yet part of the United States |
| Louisiana civil vital registration | Begins post-statehood (1914 statewide) |
| *Louisiana, Parish Marriages, 1837–1957* (FS Collection 1798054) | Starts 1837 — post-dates this period entirely |
| *Louisiana, Statewide Death Index, 1819–1964* (FS Collection 1543892) | Starts 1819 — post-dates this period entirely |

---

## Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | What it holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch.org** | Church Records 1718–1925; Probate Records 1756–1984 | Free online, indexed + images |
| **Louisiana Historical Center** (Old U.S. Mint, New Orleans) | Superior Council records; some parish registers | In-person; partial digitization |
| **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center** | Notarial acts from 1730s | In-person; some online |
| **ANOM** (Aix-en-Provence, France) | Census rolls, administrative correspondence, military records | Online portal + in-person |
| **Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives** | Sacramental registers | Contact for access |
| **Louisiana State Archives** (Baton Rouge) | State-held colonial-era documents | In-person + some online |
| **NARA** (Washington, D.C.) | Post-1803 records; American State Papers for land grants | Online (Fold3, Ancestry) + in-person |

---

## Key Genealogical Societies & Finding Aids

- **Louisiana Genealogical and Historical Society** — publishes abstracts and transcriptions of colonial-era records
- **Le Comité des Archives de la Louisiane** — specializes in French and Spanish colonial Louisiana records; has published extensive abstracts of Superior Council records
- **Historic New Orleans Collection** (research center) — manuscripts, maps, and published finding aids for colonial Louisiana

---

## Research Tips for the French Colonial Period

1. **Start with the parish register**, even if you're looking for non-vital information — godparents and witnesses often reveal family networks and neighbors.
2. **The Superior Council records and notarial archives are complementary** — a family may appear in both. Work them together.
3. **Don't ignore the enslaved-person records.** The Code Noir's baptism requirement means many enslaved individuals appear in the same sacramental registers as free colonists, sometimes with surnames or African origins noted.
4. **Cross the border into France:** ANOM is essential for immigrant origins, officer backgrounds, and colonial correspondence. Many French colonial families can be traced back to specific French provinces.
5. **Be alert to the 1762/1769 transition:** After Spain took over, the same Catholic parishes continued operating, but administrative records shifted to Spanish officials. If a family spans this transition, expect records in both French and Spanish.
6. **Published abstracts exist** for many of the hardest-to-access originals. Check WorldCat and the holdings of the **Mid-Continent Public Library (Midwest Genealogy Center)** and the **Family History Library** catalog for published transcriptions of Louisiana colonial records.

---

**Coverage gap to note:** The `external_links_search` tool returned only generic, non-Louisiana-specific links for this time window (Ancestry census, FindAGrave, Fold3, NARA). There are no FamilySearch-curated external links specifically targeting French colonial Louisiana (1700–1769) in the database — treat this as a gap and go directly to ANOM, Le Comité, and the repositories listed above.

# 🪨 Locality Research Guide: Pennsylvania Coal Mining Towns, Late 1800s (c. 1860–1905)

## Jurisdictional Overview

Pennsylvania's coal industry divided into two distinct regions, each with its own county structure and record repositories:

**Anthracite Region (northeastern PA)**
- **Schuylkill County** — formed 1811; Pottsville is the county seat; the heart of the southern anthracite field
- **Luzerne County** — Wilkes-Barre area; the northern and western middle fields
- **Lackawanna County** — formed **1878** from Luzerne; Scranton area (eastern middle field). ⚠️ **Critical boundary note:** Records for Scranton-area families before 1878 belong to Luzerne County, not Lackawanna. Work both counties as one continuous trail — the records split at 1878 but the families don't.
- **Carbon County** (Mauch Chunk / Jim Thorpe) and **Northumberland County** are also part of the anthracite belt

**Bituminous Region (western PA)**
- **Westmoreland, Fayette, Allegheny, Cambria, Indiana, and Washington counties** — less heavily microfilmed than the anthracite region; county courthouses and the Pennsylvania State Archives are the primary access points

**Immigrant workforce context:** Per Wikipedia's "Coal mining in Pennsylvania," the workforce drew heavily from Ireland, Wales, Germany, Poland, Lithuania, Slovakia, and Italy. Many immigration and naturalization records are therefore just as important as vital records for this population.

---

## ⚠️ Critical Coverage Gap: No Civil Vital Registration Before 1906

Pennsylvania **did not require statewide registration of births and deaths until 1906**. For the bulk of the late-1800s target window, **there are no civil birth or death certificates** — the record type most researchers reach for first. The FamilySearch collection *Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968* (4,218,763 records, fully indexed with images) begins right at the end of this period and will only help for deaths from 1906 onward. **Church records are not a supplement here — they are the primary vital-event source for the entire target period.**

---

## Record Types & Digitization Levels

### 1. Church Records ✅ Indexed + Images
**FamilySearch collection:** *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985* — **1,245,830 records / 982,415 persons / 607,220 images**, fully indexed and browsable. [Link](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638)

This is your most important starting point for births, baptisms, marriages, and burials in the target period. Per the FamilySearch Wiki article "Pennsylvania Occupational Records" (Coal Mining section), ethnic parishes served tightly-knit mining communities with records that often contain more family detail than any civil register:
- **Irish Catholic** parishes (dominant in Schuylkill and Carbon counties from the 1840s–1880s)
- **Welsh Congregational** and **Welsh Baptist** churches (especially in Luzerne/Lackawanna)
- **Polish Catholic, Lithuanian Catholic, Slovak Catholic/Lutheran** parishes (arriving heavily from the 1880s onward)
- **German Lutheran and Reformed** congregations

When a mining family can't be found through other record types, identify the ethnic parish that served their patch (company town) and search its registers directly. Denominational archives — the Diocese of Scranton, Diocese of Allentown, the Presbyterian Historical Society (Philadelphia) — hold registers not yet on FamilySearch.

---

### 2. Census Records ✅ Indexed + Images (external)
Federal censuses 1860, 1870, 1880, and 1900 (1890 is largely destroyed) are fully indexed on Ancestry.com and FamilySearch. The occupation column in these years reads "miner," "coal miner," or "laborer in mines," making it possible to identify whole mining households. The **1880 census** is particularly valuable because it also captures relationships to the household head.

**Mortality Schedules (1850–1885):** A supplemental census schedule listing deaths in the year preceding each enumeration, including cause of death. These are valuable for a workforce with high fatality rates. Available on Ancestry and FamilySearch.

**State census:** Pennsylvania did not conduct regular state censuses during this period — the federal census is the primary enumeration source.

---

### 3. Marriage Records ✅ Indexed + Images

Three overlapping FamilySearch collections cover marriages:
- *Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885–1950* — **524,189 records / 312,045 images** [Link](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1921317) — county-issued licenses, good coverage of the last 15 years of the target window
- *Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950* — **312,045 records / 156,023 images** [Link](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1571957)
- *Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900* — **198,765 records, no images** [Link](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1502909) — index-only, useful for locating references to earlier records

---

### 4. Probate Records ✅ Indexed + Images
*Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994* — **893,214 records / 446,607 images**, fully indexed. [Link](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1417683)

Miners' estates were typically modest but probate files often name wives, children, and siblings and can establish family structure when vital records are absent.

---

### 5. Mine Worker Employment Cards ✅ Indexed + Images + Full-Text
**FamilySearch volume DGS 002240015** — *Schuylkill Mine Worker Employment Cards, 1870–1900* — **410 images, 95% name-indexed, full-text searchable.** This is the strongest-access mining-specific record in the tool results. Covers the heart of the anthracite region's main county across the entire target period.

---

### 6. Mine Accident Reports ⚠️ Browse-Only
**FamilySearch volume DGS 002240016** — *Luzerne Mine Accident Reports, 1870–1895* — **287 images, 0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable.** Must be browsed image by image. The FamilySearch Wiki (Pennsylvania Occupational Records) notes that the **Pennsylvania State Mine Inspector Reports**, published annually from 1870, list fatal accidents with names, ages, nationalities, and cause of death. These printed annual reports are held at the Pennsylvania State Archives and many large research libraries, and they offer an indexed finding aid for the browse-only volumes.

---

### 7. Colliery Payroll Ledgers ⚠️ Browse-Only
**FamilySearch volume DGS 002240017** — *Lackawanna Colliery Payroll Ledgers, 1878–1900* — **199 images, 0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable.** Browse-only. Covers Scranton-area mines from Lackawanna County's founding onward. For payroll records before 1878 in this area, check Luzerne County holdings.

*(Note: volume_search returned 23 total results for the Pennsylvania coal region — only the first page of 3 volumes was retrieved. Additional volumes covering Carbon, Northumberland, and the bituminous counties may appear on subsequent pages; search FamilySearch directly for the full set.)*

---

### 8. Immigration & Naturalization Records — Critical for This Population
Per the FamilySearch Wiki (Pennsylvania Occupational Records, "Mining Community Sources"):
- Many miners immigrated through **Philadelphia and New York** and naturalized in county courts near the mines
- **Schuylkill County naturalization records (1840s–1906)** are held at the **Pennsylvania State Archives**, Harrisburg
- Pre-1906 naturalizations could be filed in any court of record — check Common Pleas Court records at each county courthouse
- **Passenger lists** (arriving via Philadelphia and New York) are at NARA and searchable on Ancestry and FamilySearch

---

### 9. UMWA Records 🏛️ Physical Repository
The **United Mine Workers of America** (founded 1890) maintained membership rolls and grievance files. The Wiki (Mining Community Sources section) identifies these as valuable sources for the final decade of the target window. UMWA archives are at the **Historical Collections and Labor Archives, Penn State University Libraries** (University Park, PA).

---

### 10. Military & Civil War Records ✅ Indexed (Fold3 / NARA)
Many miners in the 1870s–1890s were Civil War veterans. **Pension files** (NARA / Fold3) are among the richest genealogical records available — they often contain physical descriptions, family details, and residence histories spanning decades. Search Fold3.com and the National Archives for veterans who may have mined after the war.

**Civil War draft registrations (1863)** are also indexed on Ancestry and FamilySearch and can place a miner in a specific township at that date.

---

### 11. Newspapers ✅ Searchable Online
Newspapers.com and Chronicling America (loc.gov/collections/chronicling-america) hold many Pennsylvania mining-region newspapers from this era. Local papers routinely published:
- Mine disaster victim lists
- Immigration arrival notices
- Death and funeral notices (a substitute for missing civil death records)
- Labor strike coverage (naming organizers and participants)

Key titles to target: *Miners' Journal* (Pottsville), *Wilkes-Barre Times-Leader*, *Scranton Tribune*.

---

### 12. Land & Tax Records 🏛️ County Courthouses
County deed books and tax assessment lists are held at each county courthouse and at the Pennsylvania State Archives. For mining families who did not own land (many lived in company-owned patch towns), **tax lists** may be the only record placing them in a specific township between censuses.

---

## Key Repositories

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch.org** | Church & Town Records, marriages, probate, mine employment cards | Free online |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg) | Naturalization records, mine inspector reports, state-level records | In-person; some online via PA State Archives catalog |
| **Historical Society of Schuylkill County** (Pottsville) | Local mining company records, photographs, county history | In-person |
| **Anthracite Heritage Museum** (Scranton) | Mining-community collections, oral histories | In-person |
| **Penn State University Libraries — HCLA** (University Park) | UMWA archives and labor records | In-person / finding aid online |
| **County Courthouses** (Pottsville, Wilkes-Barre, Scranton, etc.) | Deeds, probate, naturalization filings | In-person |
| **NARA Philadelphia & Washington** | Passenger lists, pension files, military service records | In-person; online via Ancestry/Fold3 |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. Federal Census, passenger lists, city directories | Subscription |
| **Fold3.com** | Military pension and service records | Subscription |
| **Find A Grave / BillionGraves** | Cemetery transcriptions and photographs | Free online |

---

## Research Strategy Notes

1. **Start with the 1880 and 1900 federal censuses** to anchor the family in a specific township and colliery — the occupation column will confirm miners, and the birthplace column will point you to the immigrant homeland and appropriate ethnic parish.

2. **Lackawanna/Luzerne boundary (1878):** Always check both counties for families in the Scranton/Carbondale/Wilkes-Barre corridor. Pre-1878 records live under Luzerne; post-1878 under Lackawanna.

3. **Identify the ethnic parish first.** With civil vital registration absent before 1906, the local church register is your birth and death record. Find the patch town → find the parish that served it → find the register.

4. **Mine Inspector Reports as an index to accident victims.** Annual reports from 1870 at the PA State Archives list fatal accidents by name, age, and nationality — use them to locate a death date, then work backward through church and census records.

5. **Company records are fragmentary.** Employment cards, payroll ledgers, and accident reports survive inconsistently. When no company record exists, substitute: census occupation listings, mine inspector reports, union membership rolls (post-1890), and newspaper death notices.

6. **Immigration timing matters.** Irish and Welsh miners often arrived in the 1840s–1860s; Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak, and Italian miners primarily from the 1880s onward. The wave determines which passenger lists, naturalization courts, and ethnic parishes to target.
